title: Greatest hits

infobox.value: or **best-of album** is a type of [compilation album](/wiki/Compilation_album "Compilation album") that collects popular and commercially successful songs by a particular artist or band.[[1]](#cite_note-1) While greatest hits albums are typically supported by the artist, they can also be created by [record companies](/wiki/Record_label "Record label") without express approval from the original artist as a means to generate sales.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) They are typically regarded as a good starting point for new fans of an artist, but are sometimes criticized by longtime fans as not inclusive enough or necessary at all.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Greatest hits](/w/index.php?title=Greatest_hits&redirect=no "Greatest hits"))

Type of compilation album

"Greatest Hits" and "Best Of" redirect here. For a list of greatest hits albums, see [List of greatest hits albums](/wiki/List_of_greatest_hits_albums "List of greatest hits albums"). For other uses, see [Greatest Hits (disambiguation)](/wiki/Greatest_Hits_(disambiguation) "Greatest Hits (disambiguation)") and [Best Of (disambiguation)](/wiki/Best_Of_(disambiguation) "Best Of (disambiguation)").

A **greatest hits album** or **best-of album** is a type of [compilation album](/wiki/Compilation_album "Compilation album") that collects popular and commercially successful songs by a particular artist or band.[[1]](#cite_note-1) While greatest hits albums are typically supported by the artist, they can also be created by [record companies](/wiki/Record_label "Record label") without express approval from the original artist as a means to generate sales.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) They are typically regarded as a good starting point for new fans of an artist, but are sometimes criticized by longtime fans as not inclusive enough or necessary at all.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

It is also common for greatest hits albums to include new recordings, [remixes](/wiki/Remix "Remix") or unreleased alternate takes of the hit songs, plus other new material as bonus tracks to increase appeal for longtime fans (who might otherwise already own the recordings included). At times, a greatest hits compilation marks the first [album](/wiki/Album "Album") appearance of a successful [single](/wiki/Single_(music) "Single (music)") that was never attached to a previous studio album. Greatest hits albums usually are released after an artist or band's contract with a major label is completed, they have been dropped from the label, or a band member died, with next releases following on new labels.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greatest_hits_album&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The first greatest hits album was [Johnny Mathis](/wiki/Johnny_Mathis "Johnny Mathis")'s *[Johnny's Greatest Hits](/wiki/Johnny%27s_Greatest_Hits "Johnny's Greatest Hits")*, released in 1958.[[4]](#cite_note-allmusic-4) The album collected eight of Mathis's charting singles, as well as three non-charting [B-sides](/wiki/B-side "B-side") and an altogether new track. The album spent three weeks at the number one spot on [*Billboard*](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")'s [Best Selling Pop LP's](/wiki/Billboard_200 "Billboard 200") chart. The greatest hits album format then gained popularity in the 1960s and 1970s among American and British rock and pop artists. One notable example was [the Beach Boys](/wiki/The_Beach_Boys "The Beach Boys") 1974 album [*Endless Summer*](/wiki/Endless_Summer_(The_Beach_Boys_album) "Endless Summer (The Beach Boys album)"), which upon release was certified 3× platinum by the [Recording Industry Association of America](/wiki/Recording_Industry_Association_of_America "Recording Industry Association of America"). It propelled them from an [opening act](/wiki/Opening_act "Opening act") for [Crosby, Stills, Nash & Young](/wiki/Crosby,_Stills,_Nash_%26_Young "Crosby, Stills, Nash & Young") to headlining their own tour in just a matter of weeks. Some artists were even popular enough to release multiple greatest hits albums during and after their career.

Greatest hits compilations were sometimes also released as 4-track 7" vinyl [EPs](/wiki/Extended_play "Extended play"). In the late 1960s, [EMI Sweden](/wiki/EMI "EMI") released a series of greatest hits-EPs featuring artists such as [The Supremes](/wiki/The_Supremes "The Supremes"), [Ray Charles](/wiki/Ray_Charles "Ray Charles") and [Louis Armstrong](/wiki/Louis_Armstrong "Louis Armstrong").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

By the 1990s, greatest hits albums were common for popular artists, with some artists even releasing the greatest hits album as a music video collection concurrently with the album. It also became a commercially viable option to boost popularity for artists with dwindling careers. Some bands refuse to release a greatest hits album, such as rock groups [AC/DC](/wiki/AC/DC "AC/DC"), [Tool](/wiki/Tool_(band) "Tool (band)"), and [Metallica](/wiki/Metallica "Metallica"). [Garth Brooks](/wiki/Garth_Brooks "Garth Brooks") had initially refused releasing one, but he eventually agreed to it in 1994 for a limited release[[6]](#cite_note-garth-6) (the resulting record, *[The Hits](/wiki/The_Hits_(Garth_Brooks_album) "The Hits (Garth Brooks album)")*, sold over ten million copies).

In 2000, [Sony Music Entertainment](/wiki/Sony_Music "Sony Music") launched their *The Essential* series, which collects singles and other career-defining tracks of artists licensed to Sony. *[The Essential Bob Dylan](/wiki/The_Essential_Bob_Dylan "The Essential Bob Dylan")* was the first in the series, and the company has since released dozens of albums in the series with other artists under their label. In addition to artist-specific collections, the series has also released genre-specific and themed albums, such as *The Essential Christmas* (collecting pop and rock covers of [Christmas songs](/wiki/Christmas_songs "Christmas songs")) or *The Essential Australian Rock* (collecting a specific regional output). In 2005, [Universal Music Group](/wiki/Universal_Music_Group "Universal Music Group") launched a similar line, *Gold*, which collects artists' greatest hits onto two discs.

In the late 2000s and 2010s, [digital downloads](/wiki/Music_download "Music download") and [music streaming services](/wiki/Streaming_media "Streaming media") increased in popularity, which allow users to listen to their favorite tracks without the need of a greatest hits package. In 2016, *[Pitchfork](/wiki/Pitchfork_(website) "Pitchfork (website)")* said that "in the digital era, once a catalog enters a streaming service or an MP3 store, there's no need for a reissue and, therefore, there's no reason for a label to mine the vaults, searching for old music to make new again. Users can assemble their own personalized greatest hits playlists or just scan through an act's most accessed songs", which has led to greatest hits collections becoming redundant.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Despite the popularity of streaming in the 2010s and early 2020s, some artists continued to issue physical greatest hits albums, including [the White Stripes](/wiki/The_White_Stripes "The White Stripes"), [Spoon](/wiki/Spoon_(band) "Spoon (band)"), and [the Weeknd](/wiki/The_Weeknd "The Weeknd").[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) Spoon lead singer [Britt Daniel](/wiki/Britt_Daniel "Britt Daniel") said he chose to compile 2019's *[Everything Hits at Once: The Best of Spoon](/wiki/Everything_Hits_at_Once:_The_Best_of_Spoon "Everything Hits at Once: The Best of Spoon")* out of an affinity for compilations such as *[Standing on a Beach](/wiki/Standing_on_a_Beach "Standing on a Beach")* by [the Cure](/wiki/The_Cure "The Cure") and *[Substance 1987](/wiki/Substance_1987 "Substance 1987")* by [New Order](/wiki/New_Order_(band) "New Order (band)"), which had introduced him to those artists in his youth, and to provide an official introduction to Spoon's catalog for new listeners.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) [Alex Kapranos](/wiki/Alex_Kapranos "Alex Kapranos") of [Franz Ferdinand](/wiki/Franz_Ferdinand_(band) "Franz Ferdinand (band)") echoed those sentiments when describing the decision to release the band's 2022 *[Hits to the Head](/wiki/Hits_to_the_Head "Hits to the Head")* compilation, stating that "I have friends who believe you're somehow not a 'real' fan if you own a best of rather than a discography. I disagree. I think of my parents' record collection as a kid. I loved their compilation LPs. I am so grateful that they had *[Changes](/wiki/Changesonebowie "Changesonebowie")* or *[Rolled Gold](/wiki/Rolled_Gold:_The_Very_Best_of_the_Rolling_Stones "Rolled Gold: The Very Best of the Rolling Stones")*. Those LPs were my entrance point. My introduction."[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Notable examples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greatest_hits_album&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Notable examples")]

Main article: [List of greatest hits albums](/wiki/List_of_greatest_hits_albums "List of greatest hits albums")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/The_Immaculate_Collection.jpg/250px-The_Immaculate_Collection.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Immaculate_Collection.jpg)

One of the [vinyl](/wiki/LP_record "LP record") pressings of *[The Immaculate Collection](/wiki/The_Immaculate_Collection "The Immaculate Collection")*, the highest-selling compilation album by a female and solo artist.

Various compilation albums became amongst the [best-selling albums](/wiki/List_of_best-selling_albums "List of best-selling albums") worldwide with sales of over 20 million copies.[[11]](#cite_note-NewYorker-11) For example, [Eagles](/wiki/Eagles_(band) "Eagles (band)")' *[Their Greatest Hits (1971–1975)](/wiki/Their_Greatest_Hits_(1971%E2%80%931975) "Their Greatest Hits (1971–1975)")* is the best-selling album in the United States, according to *Billboard,* selling over 40 million copies.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Worldwide, *[The Immaculate Collection](/wiki/The_Immaculate_Collection "The Immaculate Collection")* by [Madonna](/wiki/Madonna "Madonna") is the best-selling compilation by a solo artist, with 30 million copies,[[13]](#cite_note-Compilation-13) and is the best-selling compilation by a female artist in major [music markets](/wiki/List_of_largest_music_markets "List of largest music markets") such as [Australia](/wiki/List_of_best-selling_albums_in_Australia "List of best-selling albums in Australia"), [United Kingdom](/wiki/List_of_best-selling_albums_in_the_United_Kingdom "List of best-selling albums in the United Kingdom") and [United States](/wiki/List_of_best-selling_albums_in_the_United_States "List of best-selling albums in the United States").[[14]](#cite_note-CompilationAustralia-14)[[15]](#cite_note-CompilationUK-15)[[16]](#cite_note-CompilationUs-16) In the UK, [Queen](/wiki/Queen_(band) "Queen (band)")'s *[Greatest Hits](/wiki/Greatest_Hits_(Queen_album) "Greatest Hits (Queen album)")* is the biggest selling album of all time. In second place is *[ABBA Gold](/wiki/ABBA_Gold "ABBA Gold")*, another greatest hits compilation which has gone on to become the longest-charting album in the UK. Queen's *[Greatest Hits II](/wiki/Greatest_Hits_II_(Queen_album) "Greatest Hits II (Queen album)")* is also one of the UK's top ten biggest sellers.

One example of a greatest hits compilation released against the artists' intentions is British rock group [the Rolling Stones](/wiki/The_Rolling_Stones "The Rolling Stones")' 1971 compilation *[Hot Rocks 1964–1971](/wiki/Hot_Rocks_1964%E2%80%931971 "Hot Rocks 1964–1971")*. The music magazine *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")* remarked that the album served as a "beautifully packaged... purely mercenary item put together by former record executives of the Stones' to cash in on the [Christmas season](/wiki/Christmas_season "Christmas season") and wring some more bucks out in the name of the [Mod](/wiki/Mod_(subculture)#Music "Mod (subculture)") Princes they once owned."[[17]](#cite_note-Hot_Rocks-17) After their management tricked the band into signing over the copyrights to their 1963–1970 song catalog, the band did succeed in changing management and major labels. However, they could neither prevent the release of *Hot Rocks* nor its successor, *[More Hot Rocks (Big Hits & Fazed Cookies)](/wiki/More_Hot_Rocks_(Big_Hits_%26_Fazed_Cookies) "More Hot Rocks (Big Hits & Fazed Cookies)")*. *Hot Rocks* remains the best-selling album of the Rolling Stones' career. [Mick Jagger](/wiki/Mick_Jagger "Mick Jagger") and [Keith Richards](/wiki/Keith_Richards "Keith Richards") continue to collect significant songwriting royalties from the *Hot Rocks* sales, but not the ownership royalties.

## In other media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Greatest_hits_album&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: In other media")]

The concept of greatest hits compilations has been adapted to other media as well. In television, some shows have released compilations of their critically successful and highest-rated episodes to drive new viewers to watch a program, such as *[Family Guy](/wiki/Family_Guy "Family Guy")*'s *[Freakin' Sweet Collection](/wiki/Freakin%27_Sweet_Collection "Freakin' Sweet Collection")* and *[South Park](/wiki/South_Park "South Park"): The Hits*. Several video game companies have re-released popular games for continued sales, sometimes with discounted prices: [Sony](/wiki/Sony "Sony")'s [PlayStation](/wiki/PlayStation "PlayStation") has released games under their [Greatest Hits](/wiki/Greatest_Hits_(PlayStation) "Greatest Hits (PlayStation)") series; [Nintendo](/wiki/Nintendo "Nintendo") has re-released games under the [Nintendo Selects](/wiki/Nintendo_Selects "Nintendo Selects") label (formerly called "Player's Choice"); and Microsoft has re-released games under the [Platinum Hits](/wiki/Platinum_Hits "Platinum Hits") label. Some video game franchises have released greatest hits collections of their own content, such as *[Super Mario All-Stars](/wiki/Super_Mario_All-Stars "Super Mario All-Stars")*, *[Sonic Mega Collection](/wiki/Sonic_Mega_Collection "Sonic Mega Collection")*, and *[Guitar Hero Smash Hits](/wiki/Guitar_Hero_Smash_Hits "Guitar Hero Smash Hits").* Film studios also have participated in the practice, often including a number of films in one package (usually by decade) under an equivalent label to 'greatest hits' through optical physical media and often timed to a landmark anniversary, and digital purchase bundles in the same vein continue to exist.

## See also
